system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

# Client Business Intelligence

Systematic approach to researching a client business, identifying opportunities, and preparing a pitch.

## Workflow

### Phase 1: Surface-Level Research (30 min)

### Source Priority (run in this order, stop when you have enough)

1. **Their current website** — identify all pages, products, about page, press page, contact. Check for `/nosotros/`, `/tiendas/`, `/prensa/`, `/blog/` — these often have buried gold (subsidiaries, partnerships, director names).
2. **Infonegocios / ABC Color / Última Hora** — PY business press. Use curl + sed for article extraction since these sites are usually accessible without JS. Search: `"{company}" director gerente dueño`
3. **LinkedIn** — `/company/{name}` for corporate page. Note: ALWAYS blocked by auth wall. Can confirm page EXISTS but can't read content. Don't retry.
4. **Google Maps** — `https://www.google.com/maps/search/{company}+Paraguay/`. Use the browser tool, then extract `[role=feed]` entries for store names, addresses, ratings, phones.
5. **Facebook** — `/pages/{name}` or `/{name}`. Usually accessible, basic info visible.
6. **DuckDuckGo lite** — `curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q={query}'` — no-JS, no-block, good for PY corporate queries
7. **Bing** — fallback when Google blocks. `/search?q={query}` with a desktop UA usually works.
8. **Corporate registry** — SET RUC (`dnit.gov.py/web/portal-institucional/consulta-ruc?ruc={RUC}`), Mercantil Registry, InfoCiudad. WARNING: These almost always return 404. Try once then move on.

### Search Queries to Try

```bash
# Ownership/directors
"{company} dueños directores familia"
"{company} gerente director"
"{company} nombre del dueño"

# Subsidiaries
"{company} Argentina filial"
"{company} subsidiaria"

# Press articles
"\"{company}\" Infonegocios"
"\"{company}\" ABC Color"
"\"{company}\" Última Hora"

# General when others fail
curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q={company}+Paraguay'
```

**For web scraping without getting blocked:**
- Use `curl -sL 'https://html.duckduckgo.com/html?q=...' -H 'User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0'` for search results
- Use `curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q=...'` for simple text extraction
- For blocked sites: Infonegocios, ABC Color, Última Hora are good sources for PY corporate news
- LinkedIn auth wall always blocks — note that the profile EXISTS but can't read without login
- Google blocks aggressive IPs — use Bing or DDG as fallback

### Phase 2: Deep Research (1-2 hours)

1. **Read press articles** — search Infonegocios, ABC Color, Última Hora for interviews with directors
2. **Extract key quotes** — director strategy quotes are gold for understanding priorities
3. **Map corporate structure** — parent, subsidiaries, strategic partners, major clients
4. **Product family analysis** — every product line, cross-sell pairs, pricing tiers, warranty differences
5. **Competitive landscape** — list direct competitors, their focus, Superspuma's advantages

### Phase 3: Opportunity Analysis

1. **Cross-sell math** — calculate the upsell potential per customer (e.g., mattress + base + protector + almohada = +Gs. 1.17M)
2. **B2B opportunities** — wholesale client portals, hotel/hospitality programs, government contracts
3. **Market gaps** — what competitors don't do that this client could
4. **Geographic expansion** — Argentina subsidiary (if exists), Brazil border cities, Mercosur advantages

### Phase 4: Contact Documentation

1. **Research the contact** — LinkedIn, news mentions, Google
2. **Document what's known vs unknown** — be honest about gaps
3. **Map authority level** — can they make decisions? Who else needs to be involved?
4. **Build relationship strategy** — warm intro? Cold outreach? Existing relationship?

### Phase 5: Pitch Preparation

Create `contacts/deal-strategy.md` with:
- Deal options (tiered from low to high scope)
- Step-by-step conversation flow (open with value → show → ask → present opportunity → close)
- Talking points (what to highlight, what NOT to say)
- Objection handling for likely concerns
- Competitive positioning (why us vs agencies)
- Pricing signals (monthly retainer, project-based, revenue share)

## File Structure for Client Documentation

```
{client-repo}/
├── contacts/
│   ├── README.md              # Index of all contacts & documents
│   ├── {contact-name}.md       # Profile + relationship strategy
│   └── deal-strategy.md        # Pitch guide + deal options
├── docs/
│   ├── business-analysis.md    # Corporate research
│   └── opportunity-analysis.md # Deal opportunities
```

### Service-at-Home Safety Protocol

When the client offers a **mobile/at-home service** (depilación a domicilio, delivery, etc.), proactively build a safety protocol addressing:

**Risk:** "Alguien pide, voy y me roban."

**Mitigation layers:**
1. **Pre-verification (call, not just text)** — Llmar antes de ir. Si no contesta, no vas.
2. **Location sharing** — Compartir ubicación en vivo con contacto de confianza.
3. **Check-in/out protocol** — "Voy a [dirección]" → "Entré" → "Salí bien". Timer de 30 min.
4. **Intuition rule** — Si algo se siente mal al llegar, cancelás. Decís "perdón, me surgió un problema".
5. **First-session filter** — Ofrecer primera sesión gratis/descuento profundo a las primeras X personas. Sirve como filtro y genera confianza mutua.
6. **No carry cash** — Preferir transferencia bancaria. Menos incentivo al robo.

Documentar en `docs/safety-protocol.md` en el repo del cliente. Incluir checklist diario imprimible y plan de emergencia (qué hacer si pasa algo). Ver `references/service-at-home-safety.md` para template completo.

---

### Phase 6: Service Expansion Research (new service line for existing client)

When a client with an existing business wants to add a NEW service line targeting an underserved segment (e.g., affirmative depilation for trans women, men's grooming, post-op care):

**Step 1: 20-Question Structured Questionnaire with Multiple-Choice Options**

Format: Markdown with 4 blocks of 5 questions each (20 total). Each question has 4-5 pre-generated options (a/b/c/d/e). The MC format forces the client to decide fast instead of rambling.

**Important: include blank "Otro: ________" lines** — clients often combine or modify options. Document their exact words, not the closest option.

Blocks are always:
- Block 1 (Q1-4): Validación personal / Motivación
- Block 2 (Q5-8): Equipo / Técnica
- Block 3 (Q9-12): Precios / Modelo de negocio
- Block 4 (Q13-16): Marketing / Contacto comunitario
- Block 5 (Q17-20): Seguridad / Privacidad / Riesgos

After the client responds, **make the remaining decisions as IA** — don't send the parsed answers back for approval. The client said "decide todo como IA". This includes: final pricing, package structures, what's included in each service, which zones get discounts, marketing copy variations. Document the reasoning for each decision.

If the client leaves a question open ("investiga online y decide"), research and decide. Don't ask again.

2. **Análisis de respuestas** — Las respuestas del dueño revelan prioridades, presupuesto, conocimientos, y miedos. Documentar textualmente, incluyendo correcciones y anotaciones que el dueño agrega a las opciones pre-generadas. Extract all "decide todo como IA" signals and act on them.

3. **Research en 7 lotes paralelos** — Dividir los 100+ temas en lotes: A) Mercado/competencia, B) Máquina/equipo, C) Comunidad, D) Médico, E) Marketing, F) Finanzas, G) Psicología, H) Logística. Investigar cada lote y compilar en un documento master.

4. **Competitor pricing via browser scraping** — Para clínicas/competidores PY:
   - Abrir con `browser_navigate` los sitios de competidores directos
   - Usar `browser_snapshot(full=true)` para capturar precios, zonas, packs
   - Extraer precios reales visibles en el HTML (ej: DepilArte tenía Gs. 49,000-220,000 visibles)
   - Si el sitio está bloqueado, probar `curl -sL` con User-Agent desktop o DuckDuckGo HTML mode (`https://html.duckduckgo.com/html?q=...`)
   - Para sitios sin precios visibles, estimar basado en: tamaño de zona (mini/pink/pequeña/mediana/especial/grande), y precio promedio de mercado PY

5. **Search engine evasion** — Google, Bing, Brave, and Ecosia all block VPS IPs after 1-2 requests. DuckDuckGo HTML mode (`html.duckduckgo.com/html`) works but also blocks after ~3 requests with a CAPTCHA. Recovery strategies:
   - Send requests with rotated User-Agent headers via `curl`
   - Use Browserbase (when available) which routes through residential proxies
   - Fall back to training data knowledge for common business intelligence topics (Paraguay market, IPL pricing, trans community orgs)
   - Accept blocked results as "not accessible via automation" rather than retrying endlessly

6. **Master document compilation** — Después de investigar todos los lotes, compilar en `docs/trans-research-master.md` (o nombre análogo) con:
   - Cada lote como sección A-H
   - Una tabla de resumen ejecutivo al final con decisiones clave (precio, descuento, marketing, plan)
   - Subir a repo y dar link al dueño

7. **100-item research checklist** — After the master document, generate a separate file `docs/{topic}-research-100-list.md` with 100 specific questions/items organized in the same categories. This serves as: progress tracker, idea source for next iterations, and documentation of research scope. Each item is 1 sentence.

8. **Website audit for existing clients** — When the client already has a live site, run a full audit covering:
   - **Structure map** — every page, component, and content file
   - **Content audit** — es.json (or equivalent): find placeholders ("****"), duplicates (two FAQ sections), missing sections (no mention of new service)
   - **UX/Design** — photos vs emoji, social proof, about section, favicon
   - **SEO** — sitemap, robots, og:image, meta tags, schema
   - **Performance** — DOM size, load time, cache headers
   - **Score each category** 1-10 with justification
   - **Prioritized improvement plan** — 3 tiers: 🔴 esta semana, 🟡 este mes, 🟢 cuando haya tiempo. Each item has: effort estimate, impact assessment, specific files to change.

9. **Action plan from audit** — After the audit:
   - Decide all pricing/package decisions as IA
   - Generate a 7-day checklist (día por día) 
   - Generate a 30-day plan with milestones
   - Generate WhatsApp messages ready to send (4 templates: new client, confirmation, 24h follow-up, 20-day reminder)
   - Push all docs to repo, give client the GitHub link

## Pitfalls

- **LinkedIn always requires login** — accept that you'll only see the auth wall. Note the profile EXISTS but can't read content. Don't retry multiple times.
- **Instagram blocks all automated access** — `net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE` always. Don't waste time retrying Instagram directly. See `references/instagram-osint.md` for workarounds.
- **Google blocks aggressively** — after 2-3 searches from the same IP, you get a CAPTCHA wall. Switch to DDG lite or Bing immediately.
- **PY corporate registries are unreliable** — SET (DNIT), Mercantil, InfoCiudad often return 404. News articles are more reliable than official registries. Always try once but don't spend more than 30 seconds.
- **Paraguay business owners often have low digital footprint** — many executives have no LinkedIn, no social media. "No public results found" is a valid finding, not a failed search.
- **Warm leads are better than cold research** — if the user has a direct contact (like Leticia Roig as GM of Superspuma), prioritize documenting what they know over searching publicly.
- **Don't rely on browser for everything** — Curl with simple User-Agent headers often works where the headless browser gets blocked. Try `curl -sL` before opening the browser for each source.
- **Infonegocios articles are gold** — they often have director interviews with strategy quotes, company ambitions, competitive claims. Extract the full article text, not just snippets.
- **See `references/search-engine-evasion-pattern.md`** for the full ordered fallback chain when Google/DDG/Bing/Brave all block automated queries. Yahoo is the most reliable VPS-search-engine as of 2026-05. Wikipedia API always works. Instagram data can be extracted from Yahoo search result snippets.

### Pitfall: Template/boilerplate leftovers

When the client site was generated from a multi-tenant builder like `paragu-ai-builder`, check for **stale template files** in `lib/client-kit/` (or equivalent). These are the generic versions of the actual components in `components/`. They are NOT used by the live site but confuse future edits. Recommend cleaning them up in a follow-up commit.

**Detection:** `find . -not -path './node_modules/*' -not -path './.git/*' -not -path './.next/*' -type f | sort` shows component files in TWO locations (e.g. `components/header.tsx` + `lib/client-kit/header.tsx`). The `components/` versions are the live ones.

## Website Audit (for existing clients)

When the client already has a live site, run a full audit covering:

### Structure map
- Every page, component, and content file. Output: tree of `app/`, `components/`, `content/`.

### Content audit
- Check `es.json` (or equivalent): find **placeholders** (`"****"`, `"placeholder": true`), **duplicates** (two FAQ arrays), **missing sections** (no mention of new service line), **stale references**.
- Check every page — does it load? Does it have content?

### UX/Design scoring
- Score each category 1-10: Funcionalidad, Diseño, Contenido, Prueba Social, SEO, Performance, UX Móvil
- Justify each score in 1 sentence
- Identify the biggest gap (typically "Contenido" or "Prueba Social")

### SEO checklist
- Favicon? (link rel="icon" in head)
- og:image for social shares?
- Sitemap.xml? robots.txt?
- Canonical URL?
- JSON-LD schema?

### Performance
- Load time (from browser performance API)
- DOM size
- Cache headers
- HTTP protocol (h3 is good)

### Prioritized improvement plan
3 tiers with effort estimate and impact:
- 🔴 **Critical** (hacer esta semana) — e.g., favicon, placeholder fix, new service section, Instagram link, photo of owner
- 🟡 **Medium** (hacer este mes) — e.g., real photos, og:image, unified FAQs, social proof counters
- 🟢 **Low** (cuando haya tiempo) — e.g., blog, dark mode, animations, chatbot

Each item should specify the file(s) to change and the exact line/region.

### Implementation after audit

When the client says "implementa todos" or "decide todo como IA":
1. Apply ALL changes identified in the audit
2. For pricing/service decisions left open by the client: research, decide, document reasoning
3. Build: `npm run build` (or equivalent) and fix any errors
4. Git commit + push
5. Docker build + deploy
6. If deploy fails (resources), note it and tell the client what manual step is needed

### Visual Artist Research

When researching a visual artist (muralist, painter, illustrator) for a website project, use `references/artist-visual-research-pattern.md` for the full ordered workflow. Key differences from business research:

- **No corporate registry** — no RUC, no SET. Use Wikidata + Wikipedia API + news sites.
- **Press articles are the richest source** — interviews reveal style, influences, quotes, career timeline.
- **YouTube is underutilized** — extraction of `ytInitialData` from HTML gives video interviews that news articles don't cover.
- **Name confusion is common** — verify with 2+ sources before concluding. Paraguayan artists may have 1-2 press articles but no Wikipedia presence.
- **Website plan is the deliverable** — unlike business research (deal strategy), artist research produces a complete website architecture (10 pages minimum) with content specification per page, tech stack, monetization strategy, and roadmap.

## Per-session reference file management

After each major research session for a client:
1. Create `references/{client}-{topic}-{date}.md` with condensed findings
2. This file is the **session transcript summary** — not a substitute for the full docs in the client repo
3. Keep it focused on: key findings, techniques learned, decisions that future sessions should know
4. Delete the oldest reference when the list gets too long (5+ files) — the repo docs are the source of truth

## `decide-todo-como-IA` pattern

When the client responds to the 20-question questionnaire and leaves decisions open with "decide todo como IA" or "investiga online y decide":
- Research each open question (competitor pricing, technical specs, community contacts)
- Take a firm decision — don't propose options back
- Document the reasoning for each decision in markdown
- Include a table: "Decisiones de la IA basadas en investigación"
- This applies to: final pricing, package structures, what's included in each service, which zones get discounts, marketing copy

## Verification

After creating the documentation files:
1. Push to repo: `git add contacts/ docs/ && git commit && git push`
2. Verify the site content reflects the research (update es.json with new company info)
3. If adding stores with GPS, add Google Maps links to the store locator page
4. Cross-sell opportunities should be reflected in product page design (show bundles)

# Brand Positioning from Repo Analysis

Derive positioning, audience, pains, and conversion copy from what a company has actually built — not aspirational claims.

## When to Use

- Founder asks for help with positioning, messaging, or landing page copy
- Company website doesn't match what the company actually does
- Need to tighten audience definition with evidence
- Writing or rewriting a homepage/landing page

## Step 1: Repo Audit for Audience Extraction

Pull all repos with metadata:
```bash
gh repo list ORG --limit 50 --json name,description,primaryLanguage,repositoryTopics,isPrivate,pushedAt | cat
```

Categorize every repo by CLIENT TYPE (not tech stack):
- Healthcare, Retail, Energy, Automotive, Education, etc.
- Note which are client projects vs internal tools vs research

### Key Questions to Answer
- Who is ACTUALLY paying? (client projects reveal real audience)
- What industries repeat? (pattern = sweet spot)
- What's the company size of clients? (SMB vs enterprise)
- What geography? (but don't limit to geography unless founder wants to)

## Step 2: Positioning Sentence

Format: "We help [specific audience] achieve [specific outcome] using [unique mechanism]"

### Rules
- Audience = defined by SITUATION not demographics or geography
- Outcome = what the client gets, not what you do
- Mechanism = what makes you different from alternatives
- Never mention tool names (no "using Claude and Cursor")
- Provide 3-4 options, recommend one, let founder choose direction

### Common Trap
Founder says "our audience is anyone who needs X" — repos always tell a tighter story. Present the evidence, let them decide whether to broaden.

## Step 3: Pain Extraction

Derive 3-5 pains from the TYPE of projects built:
- If clients had no tech team → "We don't have developers and can't afford to hire"
- If projects replaced manual workflows → "Everything is still manual"
- If clients tried generic tools first → "We tried ChatGPT but couldn't make it work for our business"
- If projects were rebuilds/rescues → "We paid for software before and it never got finished"
- If training was part of delivery → "We don't know where to start with AI"

Each pain must map to something the company actually solved in a real project.

## Step 4: Current Website Gap Analysis

Extract current site content and compare against repo reality:
```
web_extract(["https://company-site.com"])
```

Check for:
1. Hero talks about tools instead of outcomes → rewrite
2. No problem/pain section → visitor has no "that's me" moment
3. Testimonials look fake (generic names, no photos) → hurts more than helps, remove or replace with real case studies
4. Geography limiting ("serving X region") when skills are market-agnostic → drop unless strategic
5. Mixed languages in copy → looks unfinished
6. Internal goals stated as client benefits ("our vision is to be the leader in X") → remove
7. Generic service descriptions that match every competitor → differentiate

## Step 5: Landing Page Content Template

Fill this structure — every section pushes toward CTA:

### 1. HERO (above the fold)
- Headline: Main outcome in simple terms
- Subheadline: Who you help + problem + what you do differently
- CTA: Primary action (Book a call)
- Trust line: Quick credibility from real projects

### 2. PROBLEM (recognition)
- "Does this sound familiar?"
- 3-5 pain bullets from Step 3

### 3. SOLUTION (your approach)
- Reframing idea (what you do differently)
- 2-3 line explanation
- 3 key differentiators

### 4. OUTCOMES (results)
- 4-6 concrete results clients get
- Use language from actual project deliverables

### 5. TRUST (proof)
- Mini cases from REAL repos (anonymize if needed but keep industry)
- Industries served (extracted from repo categories)
- Only use testimonials if they're real

### 6. PROCESS (reduce complexity)
- 3-4 steps max
- Understand → Design → Build → Deliver+Train

### 7. FINAL CTA
- Reinforce outcome
- Remove fear/friction
- Same CTA as hero

## Rules
- No paragraph longer than 2-3 lines
- No jargon
- If it doesn't help conversion → remove it
- Every claim must trace back to a real project
- Don't use fake testimonial names — use "Verified Client" or real ones

## Pitfalls

1. **Don't let founder's aspiration override evidence** — "we serve everyone" is never true. Show the repo data.
2. **Geography is where you've been, not where you have to stay** — unless founder wants geographic focus, define audience by situation not location.
3. **Tool names in hero copy kill conversion** — nobody outside tech knows what Cursor or [REMOVED] is.
4. **Fake testimonials hurt more than no testimonials** — generic names + generic titles + no photos = instant credibility loss.
5. **Internal vision ≠ client benefit** — "position Paraguay as AI leader" means nothing to a prospect.

# Standalone E-commerce Store

Build, audit, and deploy production Next.js 15 e-commerce stores.

## Architecture

Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) -> VPS -> Traefik -> service_web:3000

## Tech Stack

Next.js 15 (standalone output), React 19, Tailwind CSS 4, Docker + Swarm, Traefik, SQLite (optional)

## Build & Deploy

```
npm run build
docker build -t <name>:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml <name>
```

## Page Structure

Home, Tienda, Producto/[slug], Envio, FAQ, Contacto, Privacidad, Terminos, Nosotros, Promociones, Blog (posts + categories + [slug]), Sitemap, Error, Not-found, RSS

## Product Data Structure (Retail)

Each product should carry rich spec data, not just name+price:

```json
{
  "slug": "titanium",
  "name": "Titanium",
  "line": "Premium",
  "category": "Resorte",
  "price": "Gs. 1.800.000",
  "priceFrom": "2 plazas 140x190",
  "image": "/images/products/titanium.png",
  "description": "Premium con Euro Pillow Top.",
  "sizes": ["2 plazas 140x190: Gs. 1.800.000", "Queen 160x200: Gs. 2.200.000"],
  "firmness": "Firme",
  "warranty": "5 años",
  "weight": "Hasta 120 kg por lado",
  "colors": ["Gris", "Azul"],
  "badges": ["Pillow Top", "Antiácaros", "Más vendido"]
}
```

Product pages render a specs grid (firmness, warranty, weight, colors), a measure/price table, and badges.

## Retail-Specific Features

- **Bundles/Combos**: Section on homepage showing 2-3 product bundles with original price, bundle price, savings, and WhatsApp inquiry link
- **Promo banners**: Downloadable from client's current site, displayed as clickable 3-column grid in `#0F1624` section
- **Store locator**: GPS coordinates per store, clickable address → Google Maps, clickable phone → tel: link, type badges (Store/Mall/Outlet/Distribución), Argentina subsidiary section
- **Product page**: Specs grid (firmness, warranty, weight, colors), size/price table, badges row
- **Cross-sell**: "Complete your bundle" section — mattress + base + protector + pillows

## Critical Patterns

- WhatsApp-first commerce (all CTAs go to WhatsApp)
- Content-driven from content/es.json
- Age-gated content (localStorage with 7-day timestamp expiry)
- Discreet shipping guarantee (for adult stores)

## Content Structure

content/es.json sections: siteConfig, navigation, home, products (14+ with id/slug/name/price/level/sound/safety fields), categories, faq, blog, footer, shipping, payments, socialProof, brand

---

## Comprehensive Site Audit Checklist

Run on EVERY page. Be brutal.

### Audit Dimensions:

1. Visual/Design -- spacing, alignment, color, images, fonts, hierarchy, consistency
2. UX/Usability -- navigation, button placement, mobile, loading states, forms, click targets
3. Content -- copy quality, tone, grammar, completeness, placeholders, length
4. E-commerce -- conversion flow, trust signals, pricing, urgency, social proof, stock
5. Technical -- load speed, console errors, broken links, SEO, titles, structured data, a11y
6. Mobile -- viewport, tap targets, readability, nav overlap
7. Industry-specific -- privacy signals, discretion tone, age gate, comfort for first-timers
8. Cross-cutting -- image placeholders, inconsistent icons, duplication, text bugs, keyboard nav

### Severity Triage:

- CRITICAL -- Broken functionality, legal exposure, blank pages. Fix immediately.
- HIGH -- Trust-killing issues (no contact info, no product images, duplicate nav). Fix this batch.
- MEDIUM -- UX friction, redundant elements. Fix alongside HIGH.
- LOW -- Polish, accessibility extras. Fix when time permits.

### Batch Fix Strategy:

1. Fix all CRITICAL first (1-3 line fixes)
2. Fix all HIGH second (users notice these)
3. Fix MEDIUM alongside HIGH
4. Fix LOW last
5. Commit + deploy as single atomic batch

### Overview of this session (Fun4Me full upgrade)

This session built on a basic skeleton store (Fun4Me, adult toys, Paraguay) and transformed it into a full-featured store matching the production El Viajero store. 25+ batches across: content overhaul, 15 pages, full search/filter/sort/pagination, adult-store privacy features (quick exit, privacy mode blur, anonymous reviews), WhatsApp commerce features, carousels, dark/light mode, PWA, structured data, SVG icons, loading states, product comparison, shipping calculator, product quiz, social proof, per-product FAQ, safety badges, sound level indicators, and a complete 520-item feature inventory.

Then ran a brutal full-site roast across all pages and fixed all 21 identified issues in a single follow-up pass.

## Brand/Creative Client Sites (Band, Artist, Portfolio)

When the client is a band, artist, or creative brand (no products, no services catalog), the e-commerce template is wrong. Build custom pages.

See `references/band-site-pattern.md` for the full pattern — content schema, page component structure, conversion from monorepo, Spotify embed, and common pitfalls.

### Personal Data Stripping for Artist Sites from Personal Repos

When building a public artist/band site from personal content (psychology repos, journals, letters):

**MUST strip before publishing:**
- Real names (family, partners, your own full name)
- Specific medical/health disclosures (HIV, medications, treatment details)
- Family role descriptions ("The Engineer", "child who wanted to be held" → "heart that wanted to be held")
- Place-specific references ("in my own home" → "in the peripheries")
- Specific biographical facts that could identify the person
- Age, location, employer, educational details

**Rewrite strategy:**
- Replace specific disclosures with universal emotional truths
- "I am not a risk / I am not a disease" → "I am not my past / I am not my shame"
- "My family didn't know what to do with me" → "I learned young that feelings were a dangerous thing"
- Keep the emotional core intact while removing identifying specifics

**Checklist:** run `grep -rni 'family|parents|mother|father|hiv|positive|diagnos|weiss|real name'` on all site files before deploy.

### Songs/Lyrics Content Pattern

For artist sites with original music, use a `src/data/songs.ts` data module instead of es.json:

```ts
export const songs = [
  {
    id: 'song-slug',
    title: 'Song Title',
    subtitle: 'One-line hook or tagline',
    lyrics: `Full lyrics as template literal`,
    genre: 'Alternative',
    duration: '4:12',
    mood: 'Vulnerable, building to powerful'
  }
]
```

Then render an **accordion/expandable lyrics section** — clicking a song title expands to show full lyrics beneath it. Use `useState` to track which song is open.

```tsx
// Key pattern for lyrics accordion:
const [openSong, setOpenSong] = useState<string | null>(null)
const toggleSong = (id: string) => setOpenSong(openSong === id ? null : id)

// Each song row is a <button onClick={toggleSong(song.id)}> that toggles a collapsible div
// The div uses animate-in: fade-in slide-in-from-top-2
// Inside the expanded panel: h-px divider + lyrics as whitespace-pre-line text
```

**CRITICAL: All interactive sections (music player, merch shop, events, lyrics accordion, contact) must be `'use client'` components** because they use `useState`, `useEffect`, or `onClick` handlers. Build them as section components in `components/sections/` and import them into the server-side page.

### PAB Token Bridge for Artist Sites

For sites following the Paragu-ai Builder design token pattern, create `src/tokens/brand.tokens.json`:

```json
{
  "colors": {
    "primary": { "value": "#D4956A", "type": "color" },
    "background": { "value": "#0D0D14", "type": "color" },
    "surface": { "value": "#1C1C2A", "type": "color" }
  },
  "typography": {
    "fontFamily": {
      "heading": { "value": "'Playfair Display', Georgia, serif", "type": "fontFamily" },
      "body": { "value": "'Inter', system-ui, sans-serif", "type": "fontFamily" },
      "accent": { "value": "'Cormorant Garamond', Georgia, serif", "type": "fontFamily" }
    }
  }
}
```

Map tokens to CSS variables in `globals.css`:
```css
@theme {
  --color-primary: #D4956A;
  --color-background: #0D0D14;
  --color-surface: #1C1C2A;
}
```

### Social Icons Row

Artist sites should include a social icons row at the bottom of the contact section with Instagram, YouTube, and Spotify SVG icons. Use `hover:text-[color]` transitions on each to match the platform brand color.

### Page Structure for Band Sites

- **Home**: Hero headline/subheadline, bio section, feature cards (discography, shows, members), Spotify embed iframe, discography cards with album art, team grid (members + role + bio), testimonials, newsletter signup, booking CTA
- **Música**: Full discography with album art per item (from Spotify CDN), track listing per album, Spotify embed
- **Videos**: YouTube embed cards — extract video ID from URL with regex `/watch\?v=([^&]+)/`
- **Letras**: Song title + album metadata, lyrics displayed as lines in a styled dark box
- **Shows**: Event cards with date, venue, description — Calendar icon per card
- **Galería**: Photo grid with category filter buttons (Todas / En Vivo / Banda / Festivales), hover overlay on images
- **Contacto**: Contact cards for each channel (WhatsApp, Email, Instagram, Facebook, YouTube, Spotify) — each with icon + label + hover color matching the platform brand

### Interactive Artist Site Sections (Beyond Content-Driven Pages)

For band/artist sites that need revenue and engagement features, build these as section components + data files:

#### Audio Player / Tracklist (`music-section.tsx`)

A tracklist with functional in-browser audio player:

**Two-phase setup:**
1. Generate demo MP3s using Python tone synthesis + ffmpeg (see `references/audio-player-pattern.md` for full code)
2. Wire the `<audio>` player component (same reference)

**Data source:** `src/data/songs.ts` — array of { id, title, subtitle, lyrics, genre, duration, mood }

**Tracklist rows:** Each track is a clickable row: play button (circle with triangle icon), track number, title, subtitle, genre, duration. Clicking expands the player panel below.

**Click interaction:** clicking a track row sets `playingId` state, which conditionally renders an expanded audio player with:
- Song info header (emoji icon, title, genre/duration/mood metadata)
- **Functional HTML5 audio player** (see `references/audio-player-pattern.md` for component code):
  - Waveform progress bar (80 bars, clickable for seeking)
  - Play/pause button
  - Time counter (m:ss / m:ss)
  - Volume slider
  - Hidden `<audio>` element with `preload="metadata"`
- "Read lyrics" link scrolling to `#lyrics` for the active song

**Streaming links:** `src/data/socials.ts` with { name, url, color } — badge-style links with colored dot, rendered above the tracklist.

**Animations:** `animate-in fade-in slide-in-from-top-2` on player panel, pulse bars on the mini-player when "playing".

**Audio file placement:** MP3s go in `public/audio/{song-id}.mp3`. The Dockerfile's `COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public` includes them automatically in standalone builds.

**Alternative to real recordings:** When no actual recordings exist, use the Python tone synthesis pattern to generate mood-matched demo tracks (bass drone + warm pad + arpeggio + soft percussion). See the reference file for the full 4-layer generation algorithm with mood-specific shaping.

#### Events / Live Shows (`events-section.tsx`)

Two-tier structure:
- Data source: `src/data/events.ts` — `Event[]` with id, title, date (ISO), venue, city, country, status (upcoming|past|cancelled), ticketUrl, ticketPrice, description, lineup, soldOut
- **Upcoming shows**: Cards with date block (day/month/year), vertical divider, venue/city/lineup/description, ticket CTA button (price or "TBA" or "Sold Out"). Sold shows get `opacity-60` treatment
- **Past shows**: Collapsible accordion (toggle button: "Show past shows (N)"). Past entries rendered simpler — date + title + venue
- Status badges per card: green "Upcoming", red "Sold Out", muted "Past"
- Empty state: "No shows scheduled yet" centered card

#### Merch Shop (`merch-section.tsx`)

WhatsApp-first catalog with no payment gateway:
- Data source: `src/data/merch.ts` — { id, name, price: { pyg, usd }, sizes[], color, description, waTemplate, image, category }
- Products grid (sm:2, lg:3 columns) with image placeholder (emoji per category + "Photo coming soon"), name, price, description (2-line clamp), size selector buttons, WhatsApp order button
- Category filter: "All", "Apparel", "Accessories", "Digital", "Posters" buttons — active state uses primary brand color
- Size selector: product.sizes mapped to small toggle buttons, first size selected by default
- WhatsApp order: builds template message from waTemplate, replaces [SIZE], opens wa.me link
- Categories in data file: array of { id, label } for expandability

#### Social Links / Connect (`contact-section.tsx` variant)

Dual-panel layout (md:grid-cols-2):
- Left: "Follow" panel — socials data array rendered as list items with SVG icon (per-platform inline SVG), name, handle, external link arrow
- Right: "Get in Touch" panel — WhatsApp card (green bg-tint with WA icon) + Email card (with mail icon)
- Social SVG icons stored inline in a `Record<string, ReactNode>` map (instagram, youtube, spotify, tiktok, x, soundcloud)
- Social data in `src/data/socials.ts`: exported `socials` array (for Follow panel) and `streamingLinks` array (for Music section)
- No markdown formatting — all JSX

### Navigation Pattern for 6+ Sections

When the nav bar has 5+ links on mobile, the layout breaks. Pattern used for Brahm:
- Desktop: horizontal links with gap-8, uppercase tracking-wider text
- Mobile: hamburger button toggles a full-width vertical menu below the header
- navLinks array: [{ label, href }] — shared between desktop nav map and mobile menu map
- Header gets `bg-[color]/90 backdrop-blur-md border-b` when scrolled past 50px (useState + scroll listener)

src/
├── data/           # Content data files (not es.json for small catalogs)
│   ├── songs.ts    # Tracklist + lyrics
│   ├── merch.ts    # Merch products
│   ├── events.ts   # Show dates + venues
│   └── socials.ts  # Social links + streaming platforms
├── components/
│   └── sections/   # Page sections (one per feature)
│       ├── music-section.tsx
│       ├── events-section.tsx

### Visual Identity for Band Sites

- **Dark theme**: `#0d0d0d` background, `#1a1a1a` sections, `#222` cards, `#333` borders
- **Primary accent**: `#8B0000` (dark red) for CTAs and highlights, hover `#B22222`

### Docker: No public/ dir Edge Case

Band sites often have no `public/` directory. Remove the `COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public` line from Dockerfile to avoid build failure ("/app/public: not found").

## Lead-to-Site Pipeline (Beauty/Wellness Template)

When generating sites from lead data (e.g., `paragu-ai-leads` repo), use the generator script + patterns in `references/lead-to-site-pipeline.md`. Key differences from e-commerce stores:

- **Pages**: Home, Servicios, Galería, Equipo, Contacto (no Tienda/Producto pages)
- **Content**: Services grid with name/price/desc/duration per category
- **Nav labels**: vary by business type (peluquería → "Servicios", gimnasio → "Planes")
- **Palettes**: Elegant Rose (peluquería), Deep Navy+Gold (spa), Dark+Red (gimnasio)
- **Dockerfile MANDATORY**: `output: "standalone"` in next.config.ts or multi-stage build fails

## Auth System: Client → Real API Wiring

When upgrading from localStorage-only auth to real API-backed auth:

### SQLite Schema (must include sessions table)

The schema in `lib/db.ts` needs a `sessions` table for token-based auth:```
## Lead-to-Site Batch Deploy Script

See `scripts/deploy-lead-site.sh` — reusable script for deploying a single lead site to VPS. Run it individually per site (not in a loop) to avoid SSH timeout on long builds:

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sessions (
  token TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  expires_at TEXT NOT NULL,
  FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id)
);
```

### Auth context patterns

1. **Always wrap children in AuthContext.Provider** — never conditionally render a fallback during loading/SSR. Children will try to call `useAuth()` and crash. Set all initial state to empty/null values.

2. **Guard localStorage** — check `typeof window !== "undefined"` before any `localStorage.getItem()` call. SSR runs this code.

3. **API helper with bearer token** — define a helper function that reads token from localStorage and attaches `Authorization: Bearer` header. This avoids repeating the pattern in every method.

4. **Auth routes pattern** — single POST /api/auth route with `action` field dispatching to login/register/me/logout branches. Rate limit with in-memory map keyed on `email:ip`.

5. **Addresses/Orders as separate routes** — PUT/DELETE on /api/addresses for CRUD, GET/POST on /api/orders. Authenticate by reading session token from `Authorization` header.

6. **Dynamic export** — all pages that use `useAuth` need `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"` to prevent Next.js 15 from trying to prerender them during build (will crash with "useAuth must be used within AuthProvider").

## Docker Build: Typescript devDep Problem

The base image `paragu-ai/next-base:node20` has `ENV NODE_ENV=production`. This causes `npm install` in the deps stage to skip devDependencies including `typescript`, which Next.js 15 needs to parse `next.config.ts`.

Fix: Set `ENV NODE_ENV=development` in the deps stage:

```dockerfile
# MANDATORY: next.config.ts must have output: "standalone"
# Without this, .next/standalone/server.js won't exist and container crashes on start
FROM paragu-ai/next-base:node20 AS deps
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=development
COPY package.json package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps
```

Keep the builder stage at `NODE_ENV=production` (devDeps already installed by deps stage).

### Cloudflare SSL Mode for New Subdomains

New proxied (orange cloud) subdomains default to **Full (Strict)** mode in Cloudflare. This rejects self-signed origin certs with error 526.

Fix: In Cloudflare Dashboard → SSL/TLS → Overview, change from "Full (Strict)" to **"Full"** (accepts any origin cert). Or generate a Cloudflare Origin CA cert.

ACME HTTP-01 challenge does NOT work through Cloudflare proxy — Let's Encrypt hits CF edge IPs, not the VPS. Use DNS challenge or temporary DNS-only mode for ACME.

### Batch Site Generation from Leads

See `references/python-site-generator-pattern.md` for the Python script pattern that generates 5+ structurally-identical sites from data dicts. Use when deploying multiple leads from `paragu-ai-leads` repo.

### Lead Site Productionizing (Auto-Generated Site Upgrade)

When deploying structurally-identical lead sites from a template (e.g., paragu-ai-leads), they often ship with:
- Only 3 pages (/, /contacto, /servicios)
- Missing HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 in Dockerfile → 504 errors
- Missing traefik.docker.network label → Traefik can't route
- No healthcheck, no non-root user
- No security headers, no robots/sitemap
- No about, FAQ, privacy, terms, 404, error pages
- Scissors icon on all service sections (template leftover even for gyms/spas)
- Generic "Transformamos tu estilo" tagline copied across all business types

**Upgrade checklist for each lead site:** see `references/lead-site-deployment-checklist.md`. Pattern: same as productionizing any MVP site — add pages + Docker hardening + SEO + content localization.

## Common Pitfalls:

### Deep Business Research
Before building any client site, run the full research pipeline — see `references/deep-business-research.md`. This includes: scraping competitor sites for all images, mapping GPS coordinates, identifying related companies/subsidiaries, finding director/owner names via press interviews, and documenting cross-sell opportunities.

### Promo carousels: badge + title + description
- Blog category filtering: accented chars need NFD normalization before comparison
- Dynamic page titles: reset on unmount to prevent stale titles
- Social proof popups: position above WhatsApp float (bottom-32)
- Age gates: use timestamp-based expiry, not boolean localStorage flags
- Stats: initialize with "---" not "0" to avoid flash before animation
- Accessibility: add skip-to-content link with #main-content target
- Filter pagination: reset page to 1 when any filter changes
- Product images: SVG files in /public/images/ exist but es.json must reference them by correct path. "✦" placeholder means the image path is wrong or missing.
- Footer social dedup: if social bar (icon links) AND footer column (text links) both point to same Insta/WA, remove the text link column.
- Title template in layout.tsx shows default even with dynamic page titles -- client components set document.title in useEffect, but SSR output still shows the default until hydration.
- Price range sliders: two stacked range inputs overlap visually when values are close. Use a single range input (max price only) for simplicity.
- Anon review forms: must have `formRef.current?.reset()` + setRating state on submit. Interactive stars need `onClick` handler and conditional `text-warning`/`text-border` classes.
- Accented category names: URLs with accents don't match NFD-normalized comparisons. Always strip combining diacritics when generating URL slugs.
- Guest mode banner: use localStorage with 24h timestamp expiry, not sessionStorage (which shows on every new tab) or simple boolean (which never shows again).
- Page metadata/layout.tsx: the `title` export in metadata is SSR-only. Client-side dynamic titles need a separate `useEffect` with cleanup.
- SVG images on product cards: the SafeImage component renders `<img>` tags. Give the container `class="overflow-hidden"` so the SVG doesn't overflow the gradient background.
- Filter sidebar scroll position: when toggling filters on/off, the sidebar scrolls to top. Acceptable limitation unless building a custom scroll-position cache.

### Roast Process (Post-Deploy Audit)

After any build or upgrade, run this systematic page-by-page audit. The full 18-point checklist from the Fun4Me session:

1. **Promo carousel**: badge + title + description rendered as one word? Badge needs `mr-1.5`, description needs `ml-2`, title needs proper whitespace.
2. **Blog content**: is the full article content rendering, or just the excerpt? If content lives in es.json, check it's long enough. If stored in a code map, verify all slugs have entries.
3. **Blog WhatsApp URL**: avoid literal template strings (`${encodeURIComponent(post.title)}` in href). Must use proper JS interpolation.
4. **Stats animation**: initial state is "0" or "—"? "0" causes flash of broken numbers. Use "—".
5. **Age gate**: boolean flag or timestamp? Timestamp with 7-day expiry is more resilient.
6. **Social proof popup**: does it overlap the WhatsApp float? Position at `bottom-32` or lower.
7. **Footer**: social platform links appear in both a column AND an icon bar? Remove the text-link column.
8. **Blog category URLs**: accented chars (Guías → guias)? Must NFD-normalize before filter comparison.
9. **Contact page**: has hours, address, multiple topic-specific buttons? Not just a single WhatsApp link.
10. **Privacy/Terms pages**: 8+ substantive sections, not 3-4 one-liners.
11. **Product images**: "✦" anywhere on product cards = image path broken or missing. SVGs should render.
12. **Page titles**: browser tab shows product name, not just site default. Check with `document.title` after hydration.
13. **Breadcrumbs**: visible on product/detail pages, not just hidden JSON-LD in `<head>`.
14. **Accessibility**: skip-to-content link present? `#main-content` target exists? Dark mode toggle has correct `aria-label`?
15. **Duplicate badges**: "Principiante" showing in both LevelBadge and SafetyBadges? Only one should show it.
16. **Price slider**: dual range inputs overlap visually? Replace with single max-price slider.
17. **Anon review form**: reset on submit? Stars are clickable? Form resets with `formRef.current?.reset()`.
18. **Guest mode banner**: shows every new tab (sessionStorage)? Use localStorage with 24h timestamp instead.

- **Skills/proficiency**: Animated skill bars with percentage levels
- **Experience timeline**: Vertical timeline with role, org, dates, and achievement bullets
- **Stats row**: Numerical highlights (markets covered, dashboards built, years experience)
- **Contact**: Simple contact cards (no merch, no events, no newsletter)

### Data Structure
Projects use a dedicated array, not es.json:
```ts
const projects = [
  {
    title: 'Dashboard Name',
    subtitle: 'Org — Department/Region',
    desc: 'What it does and what it achieved',
    tags: ['Power BI', 'DAX', 'SQL'],

    color: 'from-purple-500 to-violet-600'
  }
]
```

const skills = [

]

See `references/personal-portfolio-pattern.md` for the full component structure and theme CSS.

- Skills: 2-column bar chart with percentage labels
- Experience: Left-aligned vertical timeline with glow dots and role cards
- Contact: Centered single-column contact card with platform icons

### Build & Deploy
Same as standalone e-commerce: `next build` → Docker + Traefik, or `output: 'export'` for GitHub Pages.
